What You'll Do: Explore three different AI tools available at ASU (e.g., ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini). Give each one the same simple prompt and compare what comes back.
What You'll Produce: A short comparison notes doc: what each tool did well, what surprised you, and which one you'd try again.

What You'll Do: Given three realistic teaching scenarios (writing quiz questions, summarizing readings, drafting feedback), evaluate which AI tool fits best for each and explain your reasoning.
What You'll Produce: A completed 3×3 matrix with tool name, rationale, and one limitation per choice.

What You'll Do: Audit an AI tool your department is considering. Check its VITRA status, data handling, output quality for your use case, and accessibility. Write a one-page recommendation.
What You'll Produce: A one-page recommendation brief with sections: Purpose, Tool Evaluated, VITRA Status, Strengths, Risks, Recommendation.

What You'll Do: Have a 5-turn back-and-forth conversation with an AI tool on a topic you know well. After each AI response, ask a follow-up question rather than starting a new topic.
What You'll Produce: A screenshot or copy-paste of your 5-turn conversation with a note on what changed between the first and last response.

What You'll Do: Ask an AI to brainstorm 5 ideas for a course activity. Then deliberately push it off its initial direction: assign it a contrarian perspective, add a constraint ('no technology allowed'), or ask 'what would someone who disagrees with all of these suggest?'
What You'll Produce: Two lists side by side: the original 5 ideas and the 5 ideas after you broke the anchor. A sentence on which set was more useful and why.

What You'll Do: Use a multi-prompt strategy to brainstorm from 3 different angles on the same problem: (1) ask as a student, (2) ask as a skeptical colleague, (3) ask using a 'Tree of Thought' prompt that forces multiple parallel reasoning paths. Compare what each angle surfaces.
What You'll Produce: A synthesis doc: the 3 prompt angles, what each produced, which ideas only emerged from one angle, and your final shortlist with rationale.

What You'll Do: Ask an AI tool to recommend 3 articles on a topic you're familiar with. For each one, try to find it using Google Scholar. Note which ones are real and which aren't.
What You'll Produce: A simple table: Article title | AI said it was by... | Found it? (Y/N) | What actually exists.

What You'll Do: Ask AI to write a 2-paragraph literature review with citations on a topic in your field. Systematically verify every claim: check each citation exists, confirm the findings match what the AI said, and flag any misrepresentations.
What You'll Produce: An annotated version of the AI paragraph with color-coded markup: green (verified), yellow (partially accurate), red (fabricated or misrepresented).

What You'll Do: Design a repeatable research workflow that uses AI to accelerate literature discovery while building in verification checkpoints. Test it on a real research question and document where the AI added value and where it introduced risk.
What You'll Produce: A one-page workflow diagram with verification gates, plus a worked example showing what the AI found, what you verified, and what you caught.

What You'll Do: Ask an AI to generate 5 'facts' about a topic you know well (your discipline, your city, your institution). Identify which ones are accurate and which are hallucinated. No web searching — use your own knowledge first.
What You'll Produce: A list of the 5 claims with your verdict (accurate / suspicious / wrong) and a sentence explaining each call.

What You'll Do: Ask an AI a question where you expect it might be wrong (a niche topic, a recent event, or something with common misconceptions). When it answers confidently, apply a 3-step check: (1) Does this match what I know? (2) Can I find a primary source? (3) Does the AI contradict itself if I ask the same question differently?
What You'll Produce: A written walkthrough of your 3-step check showing the original claim, your verification steps, and the outcome.

What You'll Do: Take a real piece of AI-generated content you or a colleague produced for work use. Apply the full RACCCA framework (Relevance, Accuracy, Currency, Credibility, Coverage, Audience) and write an evaluation memo.
What You'll Produce: A structured RACCCA evaluation memo with a score or rating per dimension, specific evidence for each, and a final recommendation (use as-is / revise / discard).

What You'll Do: Ask an AI to draft a short email or announcement for a real task you have. Read it aloud. Highlight every sentence that doesn't sound like you and rewrite those parts by hand.
What You'll Produce: The original AI draft and your revised version side by side, with a note on what you changed and why.

What You'll Do: Paste a sample of your own writing (an email, a syllabus section) into the AI and ask it to analyze your writing style. Then ask it to redraft its previous output in that style. Compare the revision to your original and manually fix what it still gets wrong.
What You'll Produce: Three versions: the generic AI draft, the style-matched revision, and your final edited version. A note on what the AI captured well and what it couldn't mimic.

What You'll Do: Use a multi-pass workflow: (1) Generate a first draft with a detailed prompt. (2) Ask the AI to critique its own output against specific criteria you provide. (3) Ask it to revise based on its critique. (4) Do a final manual edit. Document how quality changed at each step.
What You'll Produce: A 4-version progression showing the draft at each stage, with annotations on what improved and what only you (not the AI) could fix.

Activity Title: Agent vs. Prompt: What's the Difference?
What You'll Do: Read the PDF section on AI agents (pages 8–9). Then have a conversation with ChatGPT where you ask it to do a simple multi-step task (e.g., 'Plan a 3-day workshop agenda and draft an invitation email'). Note where it handles steps on its own versus where you have to manually guide each step.
What You'll Produce: A short reflection: what the AI handled autonomously, where you had to intervene, and in your own words, what would make this an 'agent' versus just a chat.

What You'll Do: Pick a repetitive workflow in your role (e.g., reviewing assignment submissions for formatting, generating weekly module summaries). Map it out: what's the goal, what steps does it need, what tools would it access, where does a human need to check in? Don't build it yet — just design it.
What You'll Produce: A one-page agent design doc: Goal, Trigger, Steps (numbered), Tools needed, Human checkpoints, Risks.

Activity Title: Build and Test a Simple Agent
What You'll Do: Using a platform you have access to (Claude Projects, Gemini Gems, GPT custom instructions, or similar), actually build a simple agent from your paper design. Give it a system prompt, test it with 3 different inputs, and evaluate whether it stays on-task.
What You'll Produce: The working agent (or its system prompt + config), test logs from 3 inputs, and an evaluation noting where it worked, where it drifted, and what you'd change.

What You'll Do: Take a simple, non-sensitive dataset (e.g., a list of course enrollments by semester, a public dataset). Upload or paste it into an AI tool and ask it to describe what it sees — trends, outliers, summary statistics.
What You'll Produce: The AI's summary alongside your own read of the data. A note on what it got right, what it missed, and whether you trust its interpretation.

What You'll Do: Take open-ended survey responses (de-identified) and use an AI tool to cluster them into themes. Provide the AI with explicit instructions about how many themes to look for and what counts as a theme. Compare to your own manual read.
What You'll Produce: A comparison table: AI-generated themes vs. your manual themes. A note on which approach found things the other missed.

What You'll Do: Design and test a workflow for analyzing student data with AI that includes de-identification steps, tool-approval verification, and an audit trail. Run it on a sample dataset and document each decision point.
What You'll Produce: A reusable workflow checklist: de-identification steps, approved tool verification, prompt used, output review, and audit log template.

What You'll Do: Pick a concept you teach and describe it to an AI image or diagram tool (e.g., Canva AI, ChatGPT with DALL-E, or a Mermaid diagram generator). See what it produces and note what's usable versus what needs human correction.
What You'll Produce: The AI-generated visual, your description prompt, and a list of what you'd need to fix before using it in a real presentation.

What You'll Do: Use an AI tool to generate a first draft of a 5-slide presentation on a topic you're preparing to teach. Evaluate what it gets right about structure and visual hierarchy and what it misses about your specific audience and content.
What You'll Produce: The AI draft deck alongside your revised version, with notes on what you kept, what you changed, and what the AI couldn't know about your context.

What You'll Do: Create a complex visual (a comparison infographic or a process diagram) using AI, then systematically audit it: Are labels accurate? Do spatial relationships communicate what you intend? Could it mislead? Is it accessible (color contrast, alt text)?
What You'll Produce: The final visual with an attached audit checklist covering accuracy, clarity, accessibility, and a list of corrections you made post-generation.

What You'll Do: Locate ASU's current policy on AI disclosure for faculty work. Then find the disclosure policy for one journal or funder in your field. Write a sentence summarizing what each one requires.
What You'll Produce: A short reference card: ASU policy summary (with link), external policy summary (with link), and one sentence on how they differ.

What You'll Do: Draft three different AI disclosure statements for three different contexts: (1) a syllabus, (2) a conference presentation, (3) a grant proposal. Each should describe what AI was used for and how content was verified.
What You'll Produce: Three context-specific disclosure statements you can actually reuse in your work, each 2–3 sentences.

What You'll Do: Create a reusable decision tree for your team: given the type of work, the audience, and the institutional context, what level of disclosure is appropriate? Include edge cases (e.g., 'AI suggested the structure but I wrote every word' or 'AI generated the first draft and I edited').
What You'll Produce: A visual decision tree (or flowchart) with 5+ branches and a brief rationale for each endpoint.

Activity Title: Three Things AI Can and Can't Do
What You'll Do: Based on your experience so far (even if limited), write down 3 things you think AI can do well and 3 things you think it can't. Then test each claim by actually trying it with an AI tool.
What You'll Produce: A 2-column table (Can do / Can't do) with your predictions, what actually happened when you tested each one, and what surprised you.

What You'll Do: A colleague makes a strong claim about AI (e.g., 'AI can grade essays just as well as a human'). Draft a 1-paragraph response that acknowledges the kernel of truth, identifies the limitations, and points to a specific bias or risk they haven't considered.
What You'll Produce: Your written 1-paragraph response, plus a short list of the specific limitations and biases you referenced and where you learned about them.

What You'll Do: Design and facilitate (or simulate) a 15-minute discussion with colleagues on the question: 'What should our department's stance on AI be?' Prepare talking points that cover capabilities, limitations, biases, privacy, and pedagogical impact. Anticipate pushback.
What You'll Produce: A discussion guide with: framing question, 3–4 key talking points with evidence, anticipated objections with responses, and a proposed next step for the group.

What You'll Do: Take a routine task in your work (e.g., writing a meeting agenda) and ask an AI to do it in an unexpected way: as a choose-your-own-adventure, as a poem, as a set of interview questions. See what happens when you give AI creative latitude.
What You'll Produce: The unexpected output and a reflection: was any part of it actually useful? Did it spark an idea you wouldn't have had otherwise?

What You'll Do: Take one existing assignment from a course you teach or support. Use AI to generate 3 creative alternative versions that accomplish the same learning objective in a completely different way. At least one should use AI as a student-facing tool.
What You'll Produce: The original assignment, the 3 alternatives, and your evaluation: which one would you actually pilot, and what would you need to change to make it work?

Activity Title: Design a Novel AI Learning Experience
What You'll Do: Create an entirely new learning activity that couldn't exist without AI — not a traditional assignment with AI bolted on, but something where AI is structurally essential. Examples: students interrogating an AI character, using AI to generate wrong answers students must fix, or having AI play the role of a demanding client.
What You'll Produce: A complete activity description: learning objective, how AI is used, student instructions, what the ID needs to build, evaluation criteria, and a note on what could go wrong.

What You'll Do: For one work week, keep a simple log every time you consider using AI: What was the task? Did you use AI? Why or why not? At the end of the week, look for patterns.
What You'll Produce: A 5-day log with entries and a short end-of-week reflection on what you noticed about your own decision-making.

What You'll Do: Write a personal decision framework for when you will and won't use AI: What factors do you consider? Where's the line between 'helpful shortcut' and 'I should do this myself'? Include at least one ethical consideration and one practical one.
What You'll Produce: A one-page personal decision framework with 4–6 criteria, examples for each, and a note on what you're still uncertain about.

What You'll Do: Apply ASU's Principled Innovation framework to a real AI use case in your work: map the stakeholders, identify tensions between curiosity and care, articulate where intentionality matters most, and propose a path forward. Present it to a colleague for feedback.
What You'll Produce: A structured case study: context, stakeholders, Principled Innovation analysis (curiosity / care / clarity / intentionality), tensions identified, proposed approach, colleague feedback notes.

What You'll Do: Find and bookmark 3 reliable sources for AI in education: one institutional (ASU's AI hub), one practitioner/researcher, and one tool-focused. Read one item from each this week.
What You'll Produce: A list of 3 bookmarked sources with a sentence on what each one is good for and one takeaway from your initial reading.

What You'll Do: Collect 5 recent AI articles or posts from different sources. For each, evaluate: Is this hype or substance? What actually changed? Does it affect my specific work? Write a 1-sentence verdict for each.
What You'll Produce: A 5-item evaluation table: Source, Headline, Hype/Substance verdict, Relevance to my work (High/Med/Low), 1-sentence takeaway.

What You'll Do: Design a monthly 'AI Update' brief for your team or department: curate 3–5 items, write plain-language summaries, tag each by relevance, and include one 'try this' action item. Produce the first issue.
What You'll Produce: The first issue of your AI Update brief, ready to share, with a template you can reuse monthly.

What You'll Do: Open an AI tool and ask it: 'I'm brand new to AI. What are the 3 most important things I should understand before I start using you for work?' Read its answer critically — does it match what you've heard elsewhere?
What You'll Produce: The AI's answer, your reaction, and one follow-up question you asked it based on something it said.

What You'll Do: Pick an AI feature you haven't tried (e.g., uploading a document, using custom instructions, creating a GPT). Ask the AI itself to teach you how to use it step by step. Follow its instructions, then evaluate: did it skip steps? Was anything wrong?
What You'll Produce: A tested walkthrough: the AI's instructions, what you actually did, where the instructions were wrong or incomplete, and the working result.

What You'll Do: Design a reusable protocol for learning any new AI capability: (1) ask AI to explain it, (2) ask AI to show you an example, (3) try it yourself on a low-stakes task, (4) ask AI to evaluate your attempt, (5) cross-check against documentation. Test it on something unfamiliar.
What You'll Produce: A reusable 5-step protocol document, plus a worked example showing how you used it to learn a new AI feature, with notes on where the AI was helpful and where you needed external sources.
